content.text: In Azure, a subscription is the unit at which costs are measured, invoiced, and controlled. Every resource you deploy — virtual machines, databases, storage accounts, network gateways — belongs to exactly one subscription, and the charges that resource generates flow upward to that subscription's billing account. This is what it means for a subscription to act as a billing boundary: resource costs cannot cross it automatically.

Two subscriptions under the same billing account receive separate invoices and accumulate separate cost totals. Budgets, cost alerts, spending limits, and billing RBAC roles are all scoped to a specific subscription. A team that exceeds its budget in Subscription A has no effect on the cost view or budget status of Subscription B, even if both subscriptions belong to the same organisation.

attrs.alt: Azure Subscription as a Billing Boundary — three subscriptions each with isolated costs, budgets, and RBAC

content.text: Understanding the billing boundary requires understanding how charges flow from raw resource usage to the invoice you receive.
Resources emit usage records (meters) every few seconds — CPU seconds, GB-hours, API calls, GB egress.
Azure pricing applies to each meter to produce a line-item cost in your account currency.
Costs accumulate in the subscription's cost view in near-real-time (typically 8–24 hour delay).
At the end of the billing period (usually calendar month), charges are aggregated into an invoice.
The invoice is delivered to the billing account owner (EA: Enterprise Admin; MCA: Billing Profile; PAYG: Account Admin).
Billing Accounts and Their Relationship to Subscriptions
No container — sub IS the account
Under EA and MCA, a single billing account can contain many subscriptions. Under PAYG, each subscription is tied directly to a payment method (credit card) and is its own billing unit.

Azure Cost Management provides the tooling to observe and enforce the billing boundary. You set budgets on a subscription (or resource group, or management group), and Azure notifies you or triggers automated actions when spending crosses thresholds.
Creating a Subscription Budget via CLI

# Create a monthly budget of $1,000 with alerts at 80% and 100%
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "prod-monthly-budget" \
  --amount 1000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2026-07-01 \
  --end-date 2027-06-30 \
  --resource-group-filter-values "rg-prod-eastus" "rg-prod-westus2" \
  --notifications '{
    "actual_GreaterThan_80_Percent": {
      "enabled": true,
      "operator": "GreaterThan",
      "threshold": 80,
      "contactEmails": ["ops@example.com"],
      "thresholdType": "Actual"
    },
    "actual_GreaterThan_100_Percent": {
      "enabled": true,
      "operator": "GreaterThan",
      "threshold": 100,
      "contactEmails": ["ops@example.com", "finance@example.com"],
      "thresholdType": "Actual"
    },
    "forecast_GreaterThan_90_Percent": {
      "enabled": true,
      "operator": "GreaterThan",
      "threshold": 90,
      "contactEmails": ["ops@example.com"],
      "thresholdType": "Forecasted"
    }
  }'

# Verify the budget
az consumption budget show --budget-name "prod-monthly-budget" -o table

Beyond email alerts, budgets can trigger Azure Action Groups, which let you run Logic Apps, Automation Runbooks, or Azure Functions when a threshold fires. A common pattern is to automatically stop non-critical VMs when the actual budget crosses 100%.

# Create an action group that sends SMS and calls a Logic App webhook
az monitor action-group create \
  --name "budget-alert-actions" \
  --resource-group "rg-finops" \
  --short-name "BudgetAlert" \
  --action email ops-email ops@example.com \
  --action webhook stop-vms-webhook https://prod-logic-app-webhook-url/api/stop-vms

# Attach the action group to the budget
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "prod-monthly-budget-with-actions" \
  --amount 1000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2026-07-01 \
  --end-date 2027-06-30 \
  --notifications '{
    "actual_GreaterThan_100_Percent": {
      "enabled": true,
      "operator": "GreaterThan",
      "threshold": 100,
      "contactEmails": ["ops@example.com"],
      "contactGroups": ["/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-finops/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/budget-alert-actions"],
      "thresholdType": "Actual"
    }
  }'

The billing boundary means you can isolate costs per subscription, but within a subscription you often need finer-grained attribution — by team, workload, environment, or application. Azure Cost Management's cost analysis and resource tags give you both views.
Querying Costs by Subscription via CLI

# Show current month cost grouped by resource group
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --dataset-filter '{
    "dimensions": [{"name":"ResourceGroupName","operator":"In","values":["rg-prod","rg-staging"]}]
  }' \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity None \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"ResourceGroupName"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table

# Show daily cost trend for the last 30 days (granularity: Daily)
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --timeframe Custom \
  --time-period '{"from":"2026-06-01","to":"2026-06-30"}' \
  --dataset-granularity Daily \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table

Tags on resources and resource groups flow through to Cost Management. A consistent tagging strategy — CostCentre, Application, Environment, Owner — lets you slice the billing boundary's costs across arbitrary dimensions without creating a subscription for every team.

# Apply cost tags to a resource group
az group update \
  --name "rg-prod-eastus" \
  --tags CostCentre=Engineering Application=WebApp Environment=prod Owner=team-a@example.com

# Query cost breakdown by tag (CostCentre dimension)
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity None \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"TagKey","name":"CostCentre"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table
Billing RBAC: Who Can See and Manage Costs

The billing boundary is enforced by both Azure RBAC (resource access) and billing RBAC (cost visibility). These two systems are separate — a Subscription Owner can deploy resources but cannot see the invoice without a billing role.
Manage payment, download invoices, create subscriptions
Create subscriptions, view costs in that section
View costs and budgets; cannot modify
View and create/modify budgets and exports

# Assign Cost Management Reader to a developer (subscription scope)
az role assignment create \
  --role "Cost Management Reader" \
  --assignee "developer@example.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>"

# Assign Cost Management Contributor at resource group scope
az role assignment create \
  --role "Cost Management Contributor" \
  --assignee "finops-team@example.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod"

# List all cost management role assignments
az role assignment list \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --query "[?contains(roleDefinitionName,'Cost Management')].{User:principalName,Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table
Cross-Subscription Cost Management with Management Groups

A management group sits above subscriptions and lets you apply policies and view consolidated costs across multiple subscriptions. Cost Management budgets and cost analysis queries work at the management group scope, giving you an aggregated billing view without merging subscriptions.

# Create a management group hierarchy
az account management-group create --name "mg-platform" --display-name "Platform"
az account management-group create --name "mg-workloads" --display-name "Workloads" --parent "mg-platform"

# Add subscriptions to the management group
az account management-group subscription add --name "mg-workloads" --subscription "<sub-id-prod>"
az account management-group subscription add --name "mg-workloads" --subscription "<sub-id-dev>"

# Create a budget scoped to the management group (aggregates all child subscriptions)
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "workloads-total-budget" \
  --amount 10000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2026-07-01 \
  --end-date 2027-06-30 \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-workloads" \
  --notifications '{
    "actual_GreaterThan_90_Percent": {
      "enabled": true,
      "operator": "GreaterThan",
      "threshold": 90,
      "contactEmails": ["cfo@example.com"]
    }
  }'
Finance teams often need cost data in their own systems (BI tools, ERP). Azure Cost Management exports deliver a CSV to a storage account on a schedule.

# Create a recurring daily cost export to a storage account
az costmanagement export create \
  --name "daily-cost-export" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --type ActualCost \
  --recurrence Daily \
  --recurrence-period from="2026-07-01" to="2027-06-30" \
  --storage-account-id "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-finops/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/finopsstorage" \
  --storage-container "cost-exports" \
  --storage-directory "azure/prod"

# List configured exports
az costmanagement export list --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" -o table

Azure Free Trial and Visual Studio Dev/Test subscriptions have a spending limit — a hard cap that stops all resources when the limit is reached. Pay-As-You-Go and EA subscriptions have no spending limit by default; costs accumulate without an automatic cutoff.
Resources suspended; upgrade to PAYG to continue
Resources suspended until next month's credit posts
Charges continue; budget alerts are advisory only
Charges continue; budget alerts are advisory only

# Check if a subscription has a spending limit
az account show --query "{Name:name,State:state,SpendingLimit:subscriptionPolicies.spendingLimit}" -o table

# Remove the spending limit (Free Trial -> PAYG upgrade path)
# This must be done through the Azure portal or via the Subscriptions REST API
# Portal: Subscriptions -> <subscription> -> Remove spending limit

# Verify spending limit removal
az account show --query "subscriptionPolicies.spendingLimit" -o tsv
Billing Boundary and Resource Access: Two Independent Systems

A common confusion: a Subscription Owner can delete resources but cannot download the invoice. A Billing Account Reader can see costs but cannot deploy resources. These are different permission systems.

Azure RBAC (Owner/Contributor/Reader on the subscription) controls who can create, modify, and delete Azure resources. Billing roles (Billing Account Owner, Billing Profile Reader, Cost Management Reader) control who can see invoices, download cost reports, and manage payment methods. You must hold both appropriate Azure RBAC roles and billing roles to both deploy and govern costs.
Enrollment Account Owner (EA) / Billing Profile Owner (MCA)
Practical: Setting Up a New Subscription as a Billing Boundary
This walkthrough shows the sequence of steps to properly configure a new subscription as an isolated billing unit before any workload deploys.
Create or obtain the subscription (EA enrollment portal, az rest for MCA/EA programmatic vending, or upgrade from Free Trial).
Set the subscription display name and management group placement.
Create the initial resource groups with cost tags.
Assign Cost Management Reader to the finance team and appropriate RBAC roles to the engineering team.
Create a monthly budget with email alerts at 80%, 90%, and 100%.
Create a daily cost export to the shared FinOps storage account.
Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud (if required) at the subscription level.
Verify with az account show and az consumption budget list that everything is in place.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# setup-billing-boundary.sh — configure a new subscription as a billing boundary

SUB_ID="<new-subscription-id>"
SUB_NAME="workload-prod-eastus"
MG_NAME="mg-workloads"
BUDGET_AMOUNT=5000
FINOPS_STORAGE_ID="/subscriptions/<finops-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-finops/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/finopsstore"

echo "=== Step 1: Set subscription display name ==="
az account subscription rename --subscription-id "$SUB_ID" --subscription-name "$SUB_NAME"

echo "=== Step 2: Add to management group ==="
az account management-group subscription add --name "$MG_NAME" --subscription "$SUB_ID"

echo "=== Step 3: Create resource groups with cost tags ==="
az group create --name "rg-${SUB_NAME}-eastus" --location eastus --subscription "$SUB_ID" \
  --tags Environment=prod CostCentre=Engineering Application=Platform

echo "=== Step 4: Assign Cost Management Reader to finance ==="
az role assignment create \
  --role "Cost Management Reader" \
  --assignee "finance@example.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUB_ID" \
  --subscription "$SUB_ID"

echo "=== Step 5: Create budget ==="
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "${SUB_NAME}-budget" \
  --amount "$BUDGET_AMOUNT" \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date "$(date +%Y-%m-01)" \
  --end-date "2027-12-31" \
  --subscription "$SUB_ID" \
  --notifications '{
    "actual_80": {"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":80,"contactEmails":["ops@example.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"},
    "actual_100": {"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":100,"contactEmails":["ops@example.com","finance@example.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"}
  }'

echo "=== Step 6: Create daily cost export ==="
az costmanagement export create \
  --name "${SUB_NAME}-daily-export" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUB_ID" \
  --type ActualCost \
  --recurrence Daily \
  --recurrence-period from="$(date +%Y-%m-01)" to="2027-12-31" \
  --storage-account-id "$FINOPS_STORAGE_ID" \
  --storage-container "cost-exports" \
  --storage-directory "azure/$SUB_NAME"

echo "=== Setup complete. Verify: ==="
az account show --subscription "$SUB_ID" -o table
az consumption budget list --subscription "$SUB_ID" -o table

Azure Cost Management's anomaly detection uses machine learning to identify unexpected spending spikes within a subscription. Anomalies appear in the Cost Analysis blade and can be delivered as daily digest emails to subscription owners.

# Anomaly alerts are enabled per-subscription via the Cost Management REST API
# (Azure CLI does not yet have a first-class command for this)
SUBSCRIPTION_ID="<subscription-id>"
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)

# Enable anomaly alert for a subscription
curl -s -X PUT \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/${SUBSCRIPTION_ID}/providers/Microsoft.CostManagement/scheduledActions/anomaly-daily-digest?api-version=2023-11-01" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "kind": "InsightAlert",
    "properties": {
      "displayName": "Daily Cost Anomaly Digest",
      "notification": {
        "to": ["ops@example.com", "finance@example.com"],
        "subject": "Azure Cost Anomaly Alert"
      },
      "schedule": {
        "frequency": "Daily",
        "hourOfDay": 8
      },
      "status": "Enabled",
      "viewId": "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>/providers/Microsoft.CostManagement/views/ms:DailyAnomalyByResourceGroup"
    }
  }'

# List all scheduled cost actions (anomaly alerts + exports)
curl -s \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/${SUBSCRIPTION_ID}/providers/Microsoft.CostManagement/scheduledActions?api-version=2023-11-01" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" | python3 -m json.tool
Investigating an Anomaly via Cost Analysis

# Query for cost outliers in the last 7 days vs prior 7-day baseline
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --timeframe Custom \
  --time-period '{"from":"2026-06-23","to":"2026-06-30"}' \
  --dataset-granularity Daily \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"ServiceName"},{"type":"Dimension","name":"ResourceGroupName"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  --dataset-filter '{
    "dimensions": [{
      "name": "ServiceName",
      "operator": "In",
      "values": ["Virtual Machines", "Azure Kubernetes Service", "Azure SQL Database"]
    }]
  }' \
  -o table

# Focus on a specific resource group driving the anomaly
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>/resourceGroups/rg-suspected-rg" \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity Daily \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"ResourceId"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table
Reservations and Savings Plans Scope Within a Billing Boundary

Azure Reservations (1- or 3-year commitments for compute, storage, databases) and Azure Savings Plans (flexible hourly commitment) reduce costs but introduce a question of scope: which subscriptions benefit from a given reservation?
Shared scope: the reservation discount applies to any matching resource across all subscriptions in the same billing account (EA) or billing profile (MCA). This is the default for EA environments.
Single subscription scope: the discount applies only to resources in one specific subscription. Choose this when a team owns the commitment and should capture the discount in their billing boundary.
Resource group scope: the discount applies only to resources in a specific resource group within one subscription.

# Purchase a 1-year Reserved VM Instance for Standard_D4s_v5 in East US
# Scoped to a single subscription
az reservations reservation-order purchase \
  --reservation-order-id "<new-guid>" \
  --sku Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --location eastus \
  --reserved-resource-type VirtualMachines \
  --billing-scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --term P1Y \
  --billing-plan Upfront \
  --quantity 4 \
  --display-name "d4sv5-prod-eastus-1yr"

# List existing reservations and their scope
az reservations reservation list \
  --reservation-order-id "<order-id>" \
  --query "[].{Name:name,Scope:properties.appliedScopeType,Status:properties.provisioningState}" \
  -o table

# Change scope of an existing reservation from Shared to Single
az reservations reservation update \
  --reservation-order-id "<order-id>" \
  --reservation-id "<reservation-id>" \
  --applied-scope-type Single \
  --applied-scopes "['/subscriptions/<subscription-id>']"

# Check utilisation of reservations (should be >= 80% to justify cost)
az consumption reservations summaries list \
  --grain Monthly \
  --reservation-order-id "<order-id>" \
  --query "[].{Month:usageDate,UsedHrs:usedHours,ReservedHrs:reservedHours,UtilPct:avgUtilizationPercentage}" \
  -o table

# List all unused reservations (< 80% utilisation last month)
az consumption reservations summaries list \
  --grain Monthly \
  --query "[?avgUtilizationPercentage < \`80\`].{OrderId:reservationOrderId,ReservationId:reservationId,Util:avgUtilizationPercentage}" \
  -o table

An Azure subscription is the fundamental billing boundary: every resource's cost flows to exactly one subscription, and costs, invoices, budgets, and billing RBAC roles are all subscription-scoped. Separate subscriptions produce separate invoices and separate cost views, making it easy to attribute costs to teams, projects, or environments. Within a subscription, tags and Cost Management's grouping features provide finer attribution. Management groups extend the billing view upward, letting you budget and analyse costs across a fleet of subscriptions without merging them. Understanding the billing boundary — and the separation between resource access (Azure RBAC) and cost visibility (billing RBAC) — is foundational to designing a sound Azure environment.
